era_context: The late 15th century is characterized by the consolidation of European nation-states, the height of the Italian Renaissance, the Ottoman Empire's expansion under Mehmed II, the Ming Dynasty's stable rule in China, and the acceleration of European exploration. In Iberia, the Catholic Monarchs are consolidating power; in the Americas, the Aztec and Inca empires remain dominant regional powers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond Portuguese coastal contacts
Interior Southeast Asia and the Pacific Islands

events.title: Pazzi Conspiracy: Assassination Attempt on Lorenzo de' Medici
events.description: The Pazzi Conspiracy of 1478 was a failed assassination attempt against Lorenzo de' Medici and his brother Giuliano, orchestrated by the rival Pazzi family with the support of Pope Sixtus IV and the King of Naples. On 26 April 1478, during a religious service in the Florence Cathedral, the conspirators attacked Lorenzo and Giuliano. Giuliano was killed, but Lorenzo escaped with minor injuries. The conspiracy was quickly suppressed, and the conspirators were executed or exiled. The failed conspiracy resulted in the elimination of the Pazzi family as a political force in Florence and the consolidation of Medici power. The incident demonstrated the fragility of political stability in Renaissance Italy and the willingness of foreign powers to intervene in Italian city-state politics.
key_figures: Lorenzo de' Medici (de facto ruler of Florence, r. 1469–1492)

sources.name: Florentine chronicles and records; Medici family correspondence
Papal records and Italian city-state correspondence

events.certainty_note: The Pazzi Conspiracy on 26 April 1478 is well-documented in Florentine, Papal, and Italian city-state sources.

events.title: Establishment of the Spanish Inquisition
events.region: Kingdom of Castile and Aragon, Iberia
events.description: The Spanish Inquisition was formally established in 1478 by papal bull issued by Pope Sixtus IV at the request of the Catholic Monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella. The Inquisition was created to enforce religious orthodoxy and suppress heresy in the newly unified Spanish kingdoms. Initially focused on investigating and punishing Christian heretics, the Inquisition would soon expand its scope to include Jews and Muslims. The establishment of the Inquisition marked the beginning of a systematic campaign of religious persecution that would reshape Spanish society and economy. The Inquisition would become one of the most notorious institutions of the early modern period, symbolizing the religious intolerance and violence of the era.

sources.name: Papal bull of 1478; Spanish royal records
Historiographic consensus on the Spanish Inquisition

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Spanish Inquisition in 1478 is well-documented in Papal and Spanish royal records.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War: Continuation of Naval Conflict
events.description: The Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) continued in 1478 with ongoing naval conflicts and territorial disputes in the Eastern Mediterranean. Venice, once the dominant naval power in the Mediterranean, was gradually losing ground to the expanding Ottoman Empire under Mehmed II. The war would conclude with the Treaty of Constantinople in 1479, which would mark a significant Ottoman victory and the end of Venetian naval dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean. The conflict demonstrated the shift in power from the medieval maritime republics to the emerging Ottoman Empire.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles; Venetian diplomatic records and chronicles

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Venetian-Ottoman War in 1478 is well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration: Continued Expansion Along West African Coast
events.description: Portuguese exploration and trading post expansion continued along the West African coast in 1478 under the direction of King Afonso V. Portuguese merchants and explorers were establishing trading posts and forts to facilitate the gold trade and other commercial activities. The Portuguese were gradually pushing further south along the African coast, seeking to circumnavigate Africa and reach the Indian Ocean. By 1478, the Portuguese had established a network of trading posts from the Senegal River to the Congo River.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and merchant records

events.certainty_note: Portuguese expansion in 1478 is documented in Portuguese royal records, though specific details of individual trading posts in this year are sometimes unclear.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Consolidation Under Emperor Chenghua
events.description: Emperor Chenghua of the Ming Dynasty continued his consolidation of imperial authority in 1478. His reign (1464–1487) was marked by relative peace and stability, though it also saw the rise of eunuch power at court. The emperor's reign saw continued economic development and the consolidation of Ming control over China and its tributary states.

sources.name: Ming imperial records and court chronicles

events.certainty_note: Emperor Chenghua's reign and consolidation of power are well-documented in Ming imperial records.

events.title: Inca Empire: Topa Inca Yupanqui's Expansion and Administrative Reforms
events.description: Topa Inca Yupanqui continued his expansion and consolidation of the Inca Empire in 1478. His military campaigns extended Inca control over vast territories, and his administrative reforms reorganized the empire into a more centralized and efficient system. The construction of roads, administrative centers, and agricultural terraces continued under his direction.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers); Spanish colonial chronicles

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is uncertain; dates are reconstructed from Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions. Topa Inca's reign is traditionally dated 1471–1493.

events.title: Aztec Empire: Axayacatl's Continued Rule and Military Campaigns
events.description: Axayacatl, the sixth Aztec emperor, continued his rule in 1478. His military campaigns and administrative reforms had consolidated Aztec power, and the empire was at the height of its territorial extent. The Aztec state was characterized by a complex system of tribute collection, religious ceremonies, and military organization that maintained control over a vast territory and diverse population.

sources.name: Aztec codices (Codex Mendoza, Codex Florentinus); Spanish colonial chronicles

events.certainty_note: Aztec chronology is reconstructed from post-conquest Spanish sources and Aztec codices. Axayacatl's reign is traditionally dated 1469–1481.

disconfirming_evidence: The Pazzi Conspiracy is sometimes portrayed as a purely internal Florentine affair, but modern scholarship emphasizes the involvement of Pope Sixtus IV and the King of Naples, suggesting that it was part of a broader conflict between the Medici and their rivals. The significance of the conspiracy has been debated; some scholars argue that it was a major threat to Medici power, while others view it as a relatively minor incident that actually strengthened Medici control. The establishment of the Spanish Inquisition in 1478 is sometimes portrayed as a sudden and dramatic event, but some scholars argue that it was a gradual process that built on earlier inquisitorial institutions. The dating of the Pazzi Conspiracy is sometimes given as 1479 rather than 1478, though 26 April 1478 is the most commonly accepted date.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1478 is rich for Europe, the Ottoman Empire, and East Asia, but sparse for sub-Saharan Africa beyond Portuguese coastal contacts and the Americas. European sources are dominated by court chronicles and diplomatic records. The Pazzi Conspiracy is extensively documented in Florentine, Papal, and Italian city-state sources, reflecting the significance of the event to Italian politics. The establishment of the Spanish Inquisition is well-documented in Papal and Spanish royal records. Aztec and Inca history is reconstructed from post-conquest Spanish sources and oral traditions, with significant dating uncertainties.

graph_edges.note: Pazzi Conspiracy led to consolidation of Medici power
Establishment of Spanish Inquisition led to intensification of persecution
Establishment of Spanish Inquisition led to expulsion of Jews in 1492
Venetian-Ottoman War led to Treaty of Constantinople in 1479
